observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the following:
a. OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to conduct
periodic reconciliation of records to determine the unreconciled
balances, so that discrepancies can be immediately traced and necessary
adjusting entries be made accordingly;
b. OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries
for valid reconciling items per books such as bank memos, interest,
errors and other unrecorded adjustments;
c. Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the payees or holder in
due course to surrender the stale checks for cancellation and reissuance
of a new one;
d. OIC-Municipal Accountant to record adjustments at year end for
unreleased checks and to restore stale checks to the Cash in Bank and
payable accounts; and
e. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the
depository bank for the rectification of bank errors, if any.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to conduct periodic reconciliation
of accounts to promptly identify discrepancies and make the necessary
adjustments to ensure the accuracy and reliability of account balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete annual
physical count of PPE and include all the PPEs in the preparation of the
Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment
(RPCPPE) and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor not later
than January 31 of each year;
b. Require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to maintain and update
the Property Cards and Ledger Cards, respectively;
c. Direct the GSO and Accounting Office to fully reconcile the PPE
accounts recorded in the books to establish the reliability of the
balances of PPE accounts presented in the financial statements; and
d. Instruct the Inventory Committee to prepare the Physical Inventory
Plan (PIP) in the conduct and completion of the physical inventory and
refer to COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of the next physical
count of Property, Plant and Equipment and the one-time cleansing of
PPE account balances.

3.16 We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to designate a new
Chairman of the Inventory Committee to establish segregation of duties.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the OIC – Municipal
Accountant copies of the report of completed projects with necessary
supporting documents;
b. Instruct the Municipal Engineer and Accountant to coordinate and
regularly reconcile their records to determine the updated status and
correct balances of the CIP accounts;
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the identified completed
projects to the appropriate PPE accounts and to record the related
accumulated depreciation and the corresponding depreciation expense;
and
d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the cost of construction
materials to Construction Materials Inventory account upon purchase,
and transfer the cost to Construction in Progress (CIP) account upon
issuance.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to record, at the beginning of each
year, the RPT and SET receivables for the year based on the certified
list of collectibles submitted by the Municipal Treasurer, in compliance
with Sections 19 and 20, Volume I of the Manual on the New
Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government
Units (LGUs); and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the RPT and SET
receivable balances recorded in the books with the RPT & SET
delinquency at the end of the year provided by the Office of the
Municipal Treasurer, and make the necessary journal entries to
properly adjust the receivable accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Maintain RAAOs for the LDRRMF current year appropriation and
separate ledgers to record all transfers of unutilized DRRMF to the
special trust fund by year;
b. Reconcile the balances of the LDRRM Fund with the General and Trust
Fund books; and
c. Coordinate with the LDRRMO in monitoring and reconciling the
balance of the LDRRM Fund;

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the
Municipal Accountant to perform the following:
a. Maintain general and subsidiary ledgers under the Special Education
Fund to record all transactions pertaining to the Due to LGUs account
and post monthly all the JEVs drawn/prepared thereon: and
b. Verify, analyze and reconcile the balances between the Subsidiary
Ledgers and the Statement of Financial Position to present accurate
balances of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs Accounts in the
financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical count of inventories
pursuant to the provisions of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1
and COA Circular No. 2022-004, and furnish the Office of the Municipal
Accountant with RPCI for purposes of reconciliation of inventory account
balances in the financial statements.

8.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Supplies
Ledger Card for each inventory account and the respective offices to
maintain and record the receipts and issuance of its supplies and
inventories in the corresponding Stock Cards.

8.13 We further recommended and the respective offices concerned agreed to
submit monthly the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and Report of Supplies
and Materials Issued (RSMI) to the Municipal Accountant, in order to
facilitate the recording of expenses in the books of accounts and present
fairly the Inventory Account balances in the financial statement.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the members
of the Municipal Planning and Development Council, the Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator, the Municipal Engineer and other
Municipal Officials concerned to:
a) Ensure that priority development projects to be funded out of the 20%
Development Fund are well-planned and procurement-and
implementation-ready; and
b) Conduct periodic review on the feasibility of the implementation of
PPAs and initiate appropriate measures to ensure optimum utilization of
the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Municipal Officials concerned and the GAD Focal Point System to prepare
the Municipality’s GAD Agenda for the three-year period as required
under Item C2 par. 1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and
Item 5 par. 6 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01, to establish
the Municipality’s strategic framework and plan on gender
mainstreaming, women’s empowerment, and gender equality which
should be the basis in the preparation of the annual GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Local
School Board to submit the Quarterly and Annual Reports of SEF
Utilization to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, not later than
the 20th day after the end of each quarter and the 15th day of February of
the following year, respectively, copy furnished the local sanggunian
concerned and the Regional Office of the DBM.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Instruct the Municipal Culture, History Arts and Tourism Officer to
submit the Post-Activity Report duly received by the DILG-Iloilo
Provincial Office and DILG-Regional Office.
b. Direct the Municipal Officials and Employees concerned to submit the
lacking supporting documents.

12.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Officials
and Employees concerned to refrain from attending/participating from any
training/seminar/activity without the clear written authorization from the
DILG.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:

a. 	OIC-Municipal Accountant to apply the Perpetual Inventory Method for inventories such as food supplies and welfare goods, which records the purchases of goods and supplies through the inventory account and recognizes expenses when the items are actually issued or consumed. Also, it involves maintaining the Supplies Ledger Cards to accurately track the movement of such inventory. Since the goods were all distributed and the books were already closed, no more adjusting entry shall be made; and

b. 	General Services Officer to maintain Stock Cards and report all issuances and consumption of supplies and goods through the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSSMI), which will serve as the basis for accurately recording the appropriate expenditure accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the concerned personnel to submit to the Audit Team the supporting documents enumerated in Appendix G and to attach to the subsequent disbursement vouchers of various procurements these required documents so that the correctness and validity of the procurement could be evaluated accordingly.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to request the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) for the refund of the over remitted taxes totaling ₱346,232.15.

We likewise recommended that upon receipt of the ₱346,232.15 amount refunded or any arrangement required by the BIR, the OIC-Municipal Accountant shall make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the journal entries made on June 2021 that understated Due to BIR account and understated Cash in Bank-Local Currency account and, subsequently, remit the subject amount to the Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Require the Inventory Team to prepare the periodic physical inventory report duly reconciled with the balances of the accounting records and furnish the Auditor a copy of the report; and

Oblige the General Services Office to maintain stock cards for each item in stock to record all receipts and issuances of supplies and materials, as well as, prepare a monthly report thereof, furnishing the Accounting Office as basis to record the supplies and materials issued in the books of accounts

We recommended to submit to the Audit Team the supporting documents enumerated in Appendix G and to attach to the subsequent disbursement vouchers of various procurements these required documents so that the correctness and validity of the procurement could be evaluated accordingly.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the agency personnel concerned to exert effort in the compliance of necessary requirements as stated in the Notice of Suspensions, as well as, to settle all audit disallowances pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We further recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed that the agency be more prudent in the processing of claims for payments to avoid audit disallowances and suspensions.
For the prior years’ recommendations that remained partially implemented and not implemented, as detailed in Part III of this report, we reiterate compliance therewith.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the concerned personnel to prepare and submit to the Audit Team the Annual Accomplishment Report of the Municipality for Calendar Year 2018 and submit every January 31st of the succeeding years even without reminders so that plans and targets could be properly evaluated.

We recommended that the treasury department reconcile with the subsidiary/general ledger balance at least once at the end of each quarter, so that discrepancies will be immediately traced and necessary adjustments made.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the concerned personnel to submit immediately the required documents enumerated above so that the correctness and validity of the payment could be evaluated accordingly.  

We also recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant review and examine thoroughly that disbursements are submitted with complete and valid documentation.  The legality of transaction and conformity with applicable laws, rules and regulations pertaining to the requirements of disbursements should be complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor must seek first a written conformity and acquiescence of the Solicitor General and the written concurrence of the Commission on Audit before hiring a private lawyer and strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-008.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile real property tax records and furnish the OIC - Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis to record the Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and the corresponding Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income.

We also recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant review the recorded transactions of RPT and SET receivables and effect correction on the inaccurate balances and, henceforth, observe strict compliance relative to the recording of Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax receivables as provided for in Section 20, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs).
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to require the submission of the documents and liquidation reports relative to the transferred funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned personnel to attach to the subsequent disbursement vouchers of purchases for goods and services through small value procurement or shopping the required documents enumerated so that the correctness and validity of the procurement could be evaluated accordingly.
We recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant and concerned LGU personnel facilitate the submission of the mentioned documents needed to support the disbursements.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to review the previous journal entries made for the utilization of the trust funds. Also, a thorough analysis of the Due to NGAs/LGUs accounts should be made, segregating proper accounts and their balances even that of the prior periods. Afterwards, prelude to the conversion to new accounts, correcting entries following the discussed format as prescribed under Section 104 of the NGAS Manual shall be effected in the book of accounts.

We recommended that gasoline consumption for unauthorized vehicles used by the previous LGU management for the period January 1 – June 30, 2013 totaling ₱112,755.30 be refunded by the officials and personnel concerned.

We recommended that the documents be submitted to the Office of the Auditor in order to ascertain the reliability and validity of payments and to ascertain the distribution to recipients. 

We further recommended the submission of comments and/or justifications from the personnel concerned (such as, BAC members of the former LGU management) who undertook the procurement, as well as those who prepared the contracts (P.O.s) for the mentioned items, as to their bases of resorting to alternative mode of procurement which resulted to the issuance of several POs/contracts.

If sufficient fund warrants, we recommended that the incoming OIC-Municipal Treasurer gradually transfer the said amount from the General Fund account to the SEF account without unnecessarily hampering the financial operations of the LGU.

The outgoing Municipal Treasurer’s liability shall be determined by proper authorities as to the degree of his negligence and whether or not the act was deliberately committed.
We recommended that the LGU submit the required documents listed under COA Memorandum No. 92-751, in order to hasten the evaluation thereof and processing of the relative requests.
We recommended that the total amount of ₱185,000.00 representing rental of van in CY 2009 to 2010 and ₱30,000.00 representing rental of van without valid claim be refunded.

We recommended that DTR and Application for Leave of Absence from January 2010 to December 2011 be submitted immediately to ascertain the correctness of salaries and allowances paid to each employee of the Municipality.  Henceforth, payment for salaries, wages and allowances shall be supported with complete documentation.
We recommended and the OIC - Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the overstatement of accounts payable by ₱1.484 million.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the total amount of ₱0.592 million representing the CY 2015 PEI be refunded accordingly, as the benefit cannot be allowed in audit for void of legal basis. The granting of similar benefits for the succeeding period should be deferred until such time the said PS limitation is observed.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant and concerned LGU personnel facilitate the submission of the mentioned documents needed to support the mentioned disbursements aggregating to ₱2.320 million.
We recommended justifications by the LGU on the labor payrolls of Job Orders charged under the fund, as well as the submission, if any, of corresponding material components of the said disbursements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct:
3. The Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary accounting entries to write-off/drop from the books of accounts the items tagged as “No Description in GL/SL” with total book value of ₱20.832 million in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; 
4. The Inventory Committee to initiate the disposal of unserviceable properties with total cost of ₱2.285 million, in accordance with existing rules and regulations; and
5. The Inventory Committee to identify the personnel accountable for items tagged as “Missing” with total cost of ₱1.040 million and require the concerned personnel to settle their property accountabilities.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Treasurer to strictly impose the provision for the cancellation of the Contract of Lease in case the unpaid rent accumulated to an amount equivalent to three months’ rent.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a dumpsite that is compliant with the provisions of RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all payments out of government funds are made through checks in compliance with COA Circular 97-002. In addition, in cases where it is very difficult, impractical, or impossible to pay through check, payment shall be made by the disbursing officer in the form of cash through cash advance and not through reimbursements to other officers or employees.

3.10 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the officials, employees and others concerned to liquidate immediately their unliquidated cash advances.  Demand letters should be sent and if remain unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances, as well as seek legal remedies for those who are not/no longer connected with the Municipality.
3.11 	We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to check the propriety and validity of the claims on cash advances of those persons who are not connected with the Municipality, otherwise, discontinue the grant of cash advances.

4.6 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Review and analyze the subsidiary ledgers, identify misclassified items, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the accounts and their balances in the books; and
Ensure that transactions are properly classified in accordance with the prescribed Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units.

5.8 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to initiate the reconciliation of the unliquidated fund transfers from the source agencies, prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted, and require liquidation of the balances.
5.9 	We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive through the assistance of the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to file a request for authority to the Commission on Audit to write off identified dormant amounts, ensuring that the requirements under COA Circular No. 2016-005 are complied with.

6.7 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Engineer, OIC-Municipal Accountant and the concerned personnel to submit to the Audit Team the foregoing documents to provide thorough auditorial and technical evaluation thereof. Prospectively, complete attachments to the DVs on similar payments should be properly monitored to avoid delays in the related project evaluation.

7.6 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Engineer and the personnel concerned to maximize the utilization of the said portion of the 20% Development Fund or to fully implement the corresponding projects to arrive at the optimal socio-economic development and environmental outcomes. Relatively, the personnel/staffing constraints intended to augment the workforce for the execution of these projects must be dealt with accordingly to ensure that the latter are implemented effectively and on schedule.

8.6 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the personnel In-Charge and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit copies of the cash advance vouchers as enumerated above for post-audit by the assigned Audit Team Leader (ATL), with covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents. Further, strict compliance should be adhered to in the submission to the concerned ATL which is within 7 days after the release of the check pursuant to the foregoing Joint Circular.

9.11	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Regularly remit the amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth within the required remittance period pursuant to Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445, Section 58(A) of the NIRC, Section 6(b) of R.A No. 8291- R.A. No. 7875 (as amended by R.A. No. 9241) and R.A. No. 7742 to avoid penalties and surcharges.
Exert effort in the reconciliation of the said balances especially those that were outstanding since CY 2018 and below. Relatively, it is emphasized that printed copies of records should be maintained as part of the proper back up system.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure the fair
presentation of the cash and the liability accounts by:
a. Requiring the Treasurer to prepare a Schedule of Unreleased Checks
at the end of each reporting period and submit this to the Accountant.
b. Upon receipt of the Schedule of Unreleased Checks, the Accountant
shall post an adjusting journal entry in the books of the Municipality to
reflect the correct amount of cash and liability accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Accountant
to:
a. Request for an authority to write-off the accounts in accordance with
COA Circular 2016-005; and
b. Prepare journal entry voucher to recognize the expense portion of the
Prepaid Insurance, in compliance with existing rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
a. Direct all the Implementing Offices of the programs and projects funded by
the 20% DF to coordinate with the Budget Officer and the Accountant
regarding availability of funds allocated for the projects being
implemented and regularly submit the status report of the implemented
projects to determine the efficiency in implementation; and
b. Instruct the Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor the
implementation of programs, projects and activities funded by the 20%
DF and ascertain if these are executed in accordance with the approved
Annual Investment Plan, in compliance with existing rules and
regulations; and
c. Instruct the Budget Officer, in coordination with other offices, to provide
descriptions and specifications of the projects to be funded to streamline
the implementation process.

We recommended and the Management instruct the Accountant to:
a. Immediately submit liquidation report and supporting documents to the
SA for the ₱0.700 million fund transfer received by the agency to fund
the OTOP Express project; and
b. Submit the duplicate copies of the RCI and the RD for the utilization of
the fund transfers received, within ten (10) days from the receipt of such
reports and documents to the Auditor.

We recommended and the Management agreed require the Treasurer to stop
the practice of allowing tenants to occupy the rental units prior to the full
payment of the lease right fees and the execution of the contract of lease in
strict compliance with the Market Code of the Municipality.

We further recommended and the Management agreed to work with the
Sanggunian and consider revising the Market Code to improve the
procedure in the award of rental units as follows:
a. Execute the contract of lease upon approval of the Market Rental
Application Form to maintain full occupancy in the Market while protecting
the interest of the Municipality; and
b. Include a provision in lease contract for the settlement of the lease right
fees in accordance with the Market Code.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
a. Require the Treasurer and the Engineer determine the actual amount of
electricity expenses collectible from market block tenants and send
demand letter to those accounts with arears; and
b. Prioritize the appointment of a Market Administrator/Supervisor who
will take charge of the strict implementation and enforcement of the
regulations in the Market Code.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to capitalize the said procurement of materials by debiting the account Buildings and Other Structures and crediting Prior Period Adjustments by the total amount of ₱0.548 million, as the books were already closed.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to: 

a. 	Monitor regularly the outstanding checks and issue written notices to the payees at least one month before these checks become stale; and 

b. 	Require the cancellation or replacement of the stale checks as identified and make adjustments accordingly.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the officials, employees and non-employees concerned to liquidate immediately their unliquidated cash advances.  Demand letters should be sent and if remained unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances, as well as seek legal remedies for those who are non-employees of the Municipality.

We further recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to   check the propriety and validity of the claims on cash advances of those persons who are not employees of the Municipality, otherwise, discontinue the said grant of cash advances against the budget for Training and/or Travelling Expenses accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct all personnel concerned to maximize the utilization of the said portion of the 20% Development Fund allocated for implementation of the desired projects to arrive at the optimal socio-economic development and environmental outcomes. Such directive would involve developing strategies to address those needs in the preparation of the program of works and in the designing of several projects, such as outsourced programming which entails prudence for additional costs needed and cautions in engaging outside service providers.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to:

a. 	Ensure that distribution of stockpiled goods, such as rice, should be limited to disaster-related beneficiaries only and consistent with the intended purpose for which the fund was established. As such, the said non-disaster PPAs could be charged to AICS Fund and other appropriate funding thereof; and

b. 	Conduct a review of its current stockpiling and distribution processes for rice and other food items to ensure that they are efficient and effective in mitigating disaster risks, considering the limited shelf life of these items, and that the risk of spoilage and waste is minimized.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to facilitate the submission of the herein individual DTRs and Accomplishment Reports, duly verified and signed by the teachers’ and watchmen’s immediate supervisors to ensure that honoraria paid were based on the necessity and sufficiency of the services rendered and within the scope of their assigned tasks.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct all department heads to see to it that any training and seminars including team building activities are attended by regular/permanent employees only. If travels of job hires are inevitable and necessary, the related claims shall be charged against the Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses (OMOE) of the concerned department.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the concerned personnel to submit the accomplishment reports and DTRs of CLUP core members in support to the payment of honoraria so as to verify accomplishments as well as actual overtime rendered.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the department head/s concerned to submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Auditor for verification purposes.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the concerned personnel to submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report for calendar year 2017 so that proper evaluation could be made if GAD PPAs were effectively implemented and useful to the intended beneficiaries.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned personnel to strictly adhere with rules and regulations and submit required reports on or before the set deadline even without reminders.

We recommended and the LGU GAD Focal Point System composed of an Executive Committee and Technical Working Group agreed to adhere on the provisions prescribed under the Philippine Commission on Women (PCW)-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01 to ensure compliance in mainstreaming gender perspectives in local planning, programming, budgeting, project development, implementation, monitoring and evaluation.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
1. Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records pertaining to outstanding receivables and prepare a duly reconciled report; and
2. The Municipal Accountant to prepare a JEV to book adjustments and reflect the correct amounts of RPT/SET receivables and the corresponding deferred RPTSET income accounts based on the certified reconciliation report.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct:
6. The LVOC Nurse Coordinator furnish the Accounting Office with a detailed list of allocated vaccines received by the Municipality; and
7. The Municipal Accountant to prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to record the expenditures upon receipt of the RIS and SSMI for the COVID-19 vaccines already utilized as follows:
Prior Period Adjustment ₱5,661,431.55
Drugs and Medicines for Distribution ₱5,661,431.55

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct:
8. The Municipal Accountant to negotiate with the depositories for the early release of the bank statements and/or an equivalent report to facilitate the timely preparation of the BRS;
9. The Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit, upon cancellation of the transaction, the appropriately marked cancelled checks and disbursement vouchers to the MA for timely adjustment of cancelled checks in the books; and
10. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to issue a certified report on the reconciliation made on the over posting of receipts totaling ₱0.642 million, record the adjustments in the GL, and disclose necessary information in the notes to the financial statements about the adjustment recorded in the year.

We recommended that:
11. The designated SDO to submit the liquidation of the ICF to the COA-ICFAU in compliance with the existing laws, rules, and regulations; and
12. The Municipal Accountant to record the ICF expenses in the appropriate account based on the transmittal of the liquidation report duly received by the COA-ICFAU.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct:
13. The Treasurer to initiate the necessary procedures to return, to the identified source agencies, the unutilized fund transfers for BUB projects in the total amount of ₱227,773.48; and
14. Prepare the RCI and the RD for the utilization of the fund transfers received, within ten (10) days after the end of each month or the end of the agreed period for the project and submit these to the Accountant for proper monitoring and recording of the transactions.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately prepare the checks for the Barangay shares in the RPT Collections after the amount of shares are properly validated and ensure the release of the amount within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the stated regulation Additionally, it is also recommended that a notice if possible will be issued to the respective Punong Barangays, informing them of the availability of the said checks, thereby facilitating prompt and efficient distribution of funds to the Barangays.

We recommended and the Management agreed to strictly implement the policy on fuel consumption of government vehicles by:
17. Assigning an officer in each office assigned to monitor the preparation and submission of the Monthly Report of Official Travel and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption;
18. Directing the Accountant to verify if payment for the gasoline, oil, and lubricant for reimbursement is supported with properly accomplished DTT prior to the approval of the disbursement voucher; and
19. Directing the immediate supervisor of the requesting personnel and the Petty Cash Custodian to verify if the gasoline, oil, and lubricant expense is supported with properly accomplished DTT prior to approving the petty cash vouchers.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
20. Require the former Consultant for Solid Waste Management to refund to the Treasurer the amount paid as reimbursement for fuel totaling ₱198,000.00 for lack of legal basis; and
21. Direct the Treasurer to strictly impose the policy that reimbursement of fuel expenses shall only be allowed if a government vehicle is used and the person using the vehicle is duly authorized to perform official activities/functions.

We recommended and the Management agreed to implement a specific policy or guidelines on the grant of livelihood assistance to its constituents and the subsequent monitoring procedures to ensure that the assistance was utilized as intended.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
23. Direct all Implementing Offices of the programs and projects funded by the 20 percent DF to coordinate with the Budget and the Accounting Offices regarding availability of funds allocated for the projects being implemented and regularly submit the status report of the implemented projects to determine the efficiency in implementation; and
24. Instruct the Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor the implementation of programs, projects and activities funded by the 20% DF and ascertain if these are executed in accordance with the approved AIP, in compliance with existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LDRRM Officer to:
25. Submit a list of recipients for the 735 sacks of rice distributed during Typhoon Paeng showing the number of kilograms received and duly signed by the beneficiaries; and
26. Thereafter that all kinds of distribution in kind must be supported with proper documentation to ensure that the goods and the intended quantity of items are received by the beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Management instruct the SWM Officer to:
27. Cease the operation of an open dumpsite in accordance with Section 37 of RA No. 9003 and put up a residual waste management system;
28. If not capable of putting up a Sanitary Landfill (SLF), the municipality is encouraged to cluster up with the neighboring municipality or city for the establishment of clustered SLF or request other LGUs to temporarily accommodate the municipality’s solid wastes; and
29. Update the 10-Year SWM Plan of the Municipality to include policies that will enhance the Solid Waste Management Program of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to deposit intact all the collections daily or not later than the next banking day in strict compliance with Section 66 of GAM for LGUs, Volume I and COA Circular No. 92-382.
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to require the AF Custodian the use of RIS every time AFs are issued to AOs to ensure proper disposition, accounting, and monitoring of AFs.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
32. The LVOC Nurse Coordinator furnish the Accounting Office with a detailed list of allocated vaccines received by the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
33. Require the immediate liquidation of cash advances, especially those aging 2 years and below, otherwise, require the employees who are still in active service to refund the amount advanced, after the third and final demand; and
34. Process a request for an authority to write-off receivables especially those who were already deceased in accordance with COA Circular 2016-005.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
34. Send final demand letter for accounts due from NGOs/POs and require immediate liquidation of outstanding account balances; and

Demand letters be sent and if remained unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances, as well as seek legal remedies for those who are non-employees of the Municipal Government of San Enrique.

Also, attach a certification stating that previous cash advance of the concerned accountable officer was already liquidated.

Review available records and reconstruct subsidiary ledger for Due from LGUs in the total amount of ₱2,712,200.63;

Issue a memorandum which will require the adoption of a uniform property identification system for PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct reconciliation of PPE per physical count and per GL and make necessary adjustment in accordance with existing laws and regulations.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create a Disposal Committee who will be tasked to handle the disposal of unserviceable properties in accordance with existing laws and regulations.

General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile, update the property cards/ledgers and act promptly on discrepancies noted in the conduct of the periodic physical count of property, plant and equipment in compliance with Item 6.3 of COA Circular No. 2020-006;
General Services Officer maintain records of Other Land Improvements, Infrastructure Assets particularly on road networks, water supply systems, parks, plazas and monuments as well as Other Structures, various equipment and motor vehicles and coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile these PPE; and
Municipal Mayor initiate that the useful lives and residual values of PPE without valuation are assessed in accordance with accounting rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and officials concerned review the existing controls in the management of collections and deposits and monitor its proper implementation to ensure sound internal control over cash.

We also recommended that the treasury department:

Record daily all transactions in the cash book,
Reconcile the cashbook balance on collections and deposits with cash on hand daily,
Reconcile with the subsidiary/general ledger balance at least once at the end of each quarter, so that discrepancies will be immediately traced and necessary adjustments made.
	
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor enforce strict adherence with the provisions of Section 69 of   P.D. 1445 and Section 32 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I, by instructing the Municipal Treasurer to deposit with the proper government depository the full amount of the collections not later than the following banking day to avoid the risk of loss and/or misuse of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local School Board to submit to the Office of the City Auditor the corresponding Registry of Qualified Applicants (RQA) and the teacher deployment analysis of DepEd.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to submit the CY 2017 Statement of Appropriations, Allotment and Obligations (SAAOB) for the Special Education Fund (SEF) and its supplemental budget.

We likewise recommended that the Local School Board prepare the budget of the SEF in accordance with the provisions of DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and include in the budget the list of projects/activities that could be charged against the Special Education Fund pursuant to the mentioned laws and regulations; otherwise, such expenses should be charged against the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, OIC - Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to effect the complete and immediate submission of the lacking disbursement vouchers and supporting documents. 

We likewise recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure strict compliance with the aforesaid laws, rules and regulations and closely monitor the submission of the reports and supporting documents by the accountable officers for immediate recording in the books of accounts so as to facilitate submission to the auditor within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the department head/s concerned:

To ensure that the use of government motor vehicles shall be authorized only through the issuance of a properly accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets; 

To submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Auditor for verification purposes.


We likewise recommended that the accounting unit review and examine thoroughly the required supporting documents for the payment of fuel, oil and lubricants to ascertain the correctness and reasonableness of fuel consumption.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Development Council, Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) to prepare a monitoring schedule so as to prioritize the timely and full implementation of all programs/projects/ activities funded from the 20% Development Fund.

We further recommended that in the formulation of the Annual Investment Plan, only those programs/projects/ activities that partake the nature of an investment or capital expenditure are included.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the agency personnel concerned to exert effort in the compliance of necessary requirements as stated in the Notice of Suspensions, as well as, to settle all audit disallowances pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended further that the agency be more prudent in the processing of claims for payments to avoid audit disallowances and suspensions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Bids and Awards Committee strictly comply with R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations.  

We likewise recommended that disbursements be paid directly to suppliers so that taxes are withheld and deducted to provide the government of additional revenue. 

We also recommended that meals and snacks served to guests or visitors and during monthly meetings/conferences are proper charges against representation allowance of the concerned officials/employees.

We recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant should evaluate the nature and effect of the transactions, determine the necessary adjusting entries and record the same in the books to reflect the correct balances of the accounts.  He should see to it that expenses incurred but not paid in prior years must be recorded in the books as accounts payable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all officials and employees concerned to liquidate or refund immediately the cash advances granted them.  Demand letters should be sent to the concerned officials and employees.  If remained unheeded, the Municipal Mayor may cause or order the suspension of payment of their salaries until they have fully liquidated thereof.
We also recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant see to it that cash advances are not granted to officials and employees with previous cash accountabilities in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations and to attach a certification on the grant of a new cash advance that previous cash advances of the concerned accountable officers were liquidated.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant and the MDRRM Officer to prepare and submit each month a Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the Audit Team of the municipality.

We also recommended strict adherence with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and regular submission to the COA Audit Team of the municipality’s Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the responsible personnel to give priority and fast-track the processing of the titles of these lots, including the identification of their original donors or owners. 

We also recommended the provision of pertinent budget, such as that of the 20% development fund, to hasten the titling of these real properties in the name of the municipality in order to protect them from possible third party claims.

We recommended that:

1.    Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption (MRFC) for the period January 1 to December 31, 2015 be submitted to enable the Auditor to determine the reasonableness of fuel consumption. Consequently, MCRF should be regularly prepared and submitted as required;
2.    Driver’s Trip Tickets be accomplished properly to prove the correctness/accuracy of fuel consumed; and
3.    Officials and employees concerned refund the amount of ₱165,943.89 which is considered irregular expenditures pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant review the previous journal entries made for the utilization of the trust funds. Also, a thorough analysis of the Due to NGAs/LGUs accounts should be made, segregating proper accounts and their balances even that of the prior periods.  Afterwards, prelude to the conversion to new accounts, correcting entries following the discussed format as prescribed under Section 104 of the NGAS Manual shall be effected in the book of accounts.

We recommended that gasoline consumption for unauthorized vehicles used by the previous LGU management for the period January 1 – June 30, 2013 totaling ₱112,755.30 be refunded by the officials and personnel concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant verify the status of the outstanding checks with their payees and make necessary adjustments in the books for the accounts affected, such as but not limited to, Due to NGAs, Due to Other LGUs, and Accounts Payable.

We recommended that the LGU management institute measures that would bring the monthly net	take home pay to the minimum amount set by the General Appropriations Act. This may include prioritizing in the payrolls those deductions and contributions mandated by law and ensuring full payment of certain loans before securing another.

We recommended that LGU management require the Disbursing Officer to stamp all Disbursement Vouchers including their supporting documents with “PAID” pursuant to sound internal controls over disbursements.

We recommended that the LGU submit the required documents listed under COA Memorandum No. 92-751 dated February 24, 1992, in order to hasten the evaluation thereof and processing of the relative requests.

We recommended that the total amount of ₱185,000.00 representing rental of van in CY 2009 to 2010 and ₱30,000.00 representing rental of van without valid claim be refunded.

Henceforth, direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant shall see that all disbursements are covered by contract to establish the validity and correctness of the claim to avoid disallowance.  Likewise, take up as accounts payable claims not paid in the year they are incurred.

We recommended that DTR and Application for Leave of Absence from January 2010 to December 2011 be submitted immediately to ascertain the correctness of salaries and allowances paid to each employee of the municipality.  Henceforth, payment for salaries, wages and allowances shall be supported with complete documentation.
We recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the overstatement of accounts payable by ₱1,484,129.41.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the assigned department head/s or personnel to fast track the submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI), attached as “Annex VIII” of the CY 2014 Annual Audit Report, to the auditor concerned to facilitate monitoring and validations of the actions taken by the LGU on the related audit observations and recommendations, and eventually to submit a validation report to the Regional Director, COA Region VI, for monitoring purpose.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements and submit the same to the Auditor within the reglementary period. In view thereof, Bank Statements should be secured immediately. A request may be made to the depository bank to furnish the Office of the Municipal Accountant with the Bank Statements including debit/credit memos (DM/CM), paid checks, and the like, within five (5) days after the end of each month pursuant to Item 3.1 of COA Circular No. 96-011. The LGU should also make representations with the bank to address the issue of delayed arrival of bank statements. 

Furthermore, as it warrants and LGU resources are adequate, manpower in the Accounting Office should be augmented in order to expedite preparation and submission of reports, such as banks reconciliation statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Inventory Committee of the municipality to exert effort to conduct the physical count of all PPEs and then submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) as of December 31, 2016.

We likewise recommended that the Accounting Office reconcile its record with the General Services Office in order to have a correct presentation of the account in the financial statements.  Any unrecorded PPE must be recorded in the books while recorded PPE which do not exist should be verified.  In case of loss, the Accountable Officer or the person to whom the property was issued should be held liable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC - Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer to discontinue the practice of charging prior years’ expenditure to current year appropriations and that proper matching of expenses against appropriation be always observed pursuant to the cited regulations. 
 In addition, the OIC - Municipal Accountant should evaluate the nature and effect of the transactions, determine the necessary adjusting entries and record the same in the books to reflect the correct balances of the accounts.  He should see to it that expenses incurred but not paid in prior years must be recorded in the books as accounts payable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and OIC – Municipal Accountant the complete and immediate submission of the lacking disbursement vouchers and supporting documents. 


We likewise recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure strict compliance with the aforesaid laws, rules and regulations and monitor the submission of the reports and supporting documents by the accountable officers for immediate recording in the books of accounts so as to facilitate submission to the Auditor within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile real property tax records and furnish the OIC-Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis to record the Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and the corresponding Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income.

We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant review the recorded transactions of RPT and SET receivables and effect correction on the inaccurate balances and, henceforth, observe strict compliance relative to the recording of Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax receivables as provided for in Section 20, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop the practice of procuring supplies, materials, meals and snacks and spare parts on reimbursement basis except for exceptional and emergency cases. Procurement procedures as mandated under Republic Act 9184 should be strictly followed.


We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer set up petty cash fund for payment of petty operating expenses.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Budget Officer and OIC - Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 so as to make funds available for the delivery of basic services and development projects of the municipality for the benefit of its residents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to require the submission of the documents and liquidation reports relative to the transferred funds.


We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to stop granting additional fund transfers to barangays with outstanding balances without demanding the liquidations of the previous ones.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned personnel to attach to the subsequent disbursement vouchers of purchases for goods and services through small value procurement or shopping the required documents enumerated so that the correctness and validity of the procurement could be evaluated accordingly.

Likewise, all supporting documents that were machine copied must be duly certified as such.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum No. 2011-1 is properly observed especially on the use of 20% Development Fund, so that appropriate utilization and implementation of the developmental projects is carried out effectively and efficiently and to discontinue from charging similar expenses that have no capital expenditure property or components. We further recommended justifications by the LGU on the labor payrolls of Job Orders charged under the fund, as well as proof/summary, if any, of corresponding material components of the said disbursements.


We also recommended that those projects/programs with general use or having administrative nature must be appropriated/funded out of the General Fund.

We recommend that the total amount of ₱592,000.00 representing the CY 2015 PEI be refunded accordingly, as the benefit cannot be allowed in audit for void of legal basis. We recommend that the OIC-Municipal Accountant and concerned LGU personnel facilitate the submission of the mentioned documents needed to support the mentioned disbursements aggregating to ₱2,320,178.81.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant review the previous journal entries made for the utilization of the trust funds. We recommend that the municipality refrain from charging similar expenses with no capital expenditure property or components or of administrative or regular activities from the 20% Development Fund to ensure that concrete, meaningful development projects, program and activities could be realized with long-term benefits.

Also, we recommend that those projects/programs with general use or having administrative nature may be appropriated/funded out of the General Fund Proper.

We recommend that the documents be submitted to the Office of the Auditor in order to ascertain the reliability and validity of payments and to ascertain the distribution to recipients. 

We further recommend the submission of comments and/or justifications from the personnel concerned (such as, BAC members of the former LGU management) who undertook the procurement, as well as those who prepared the contracts (P.O.s) for the mentioned items, as to their bases of resorting to alternative mode of procurement which resulted to the issuance of several PO.s/contracts.

If sufficient fund warrants, we recommend the incoming OIC-Municipal Treasurer to gradually transfer the said amount from the General Fund account to the SEF account without unnecessarily hampering the financial operations of the LGU.

(Cashbook/General/Subsidiary Ledgers) at least every end of each quarter and any items

in vault balance or the Accountable Officer‟s balance of accountability at any given time

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections intact daily or
not later than the next banking day pursuant to Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs
Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor demand immediate liquidation/settlement of
unliquidated CA pursuant to Section 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Management allocate sufficient funds to provide the
necessary equipment and manpower in order for the SWM Office to implement an
effective and efficient SWM System in compliance with RA 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all payments out of
government funds are made through checks in compliance with COA Circular 97-002. In
cases where it is very difficult, impractical or impossible to pay through check, payment
shall be made by the disbursing officer in the form of cash through cash advance and not
through reimbursements to other officer or employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit monthly BRS for the 17
bank accounts within 10 days after the end of each month in compliance with Section
3.4 of COA Circular 96-011.

supporting documents.

within the first 10 days of the ensuing month in compliance with COA Circular No.
2009-006.

Once the depreciation for prior years’ have been recorded in the books of the municipality, the current year’s depreciation shall be taken up monthly.

Depreciation expenses should be recorded periodically in compliance with the regulations and for the purpose of systematic and rational allocation of non-cash expenses over the assets’ useful lives. Proper recording and valuation of these assets would provide meaningful presentation of PPE accounts in the LGU’s financial statements.

We recommended that the members of the LSB, in the preparation of the LSB Budget should give priority to programs, activities and projects enumerated in Section 363(c) of the IRR of RA 7160 but the amount noted was and must utilize the proceeds of the additional one percent Special Education Fund to such purpose prescribed by law for which SEF can be disposed of.

We further recommended that the LSB refrain from utilizing the SEF fund for payment of expenses that are not priority program, projects and activities in compliance to section 363(c) of the IRR of RA 7160.
observations.reason: There were still expenditures charged to the SEF were not among or within the items allowed minimal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management allocate sufficient funds to provide the necessary equipment and manpower for sound solid waste management. We further recommended that the SWM Board develops and adopts efficient SWM policies integrated up to the barangay level in compliance with RA 9003.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Reiterated in current year‟s audit findings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Government shall formulate adequate PPA in their GAD Plan and Budget as well as appropriate and utilized the minimum budgetary requirement for Gender and Development of five percent (5%) of the total budget for the year.
observations.reason: GAD related reports were not submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that, in order to validate the grant of PRAISE Incentives, the Management submit the following documents:
a. List of Savings and Monetary Awards earned/generated during CY 2015 and relevant supporting documents/computations; and
b. Evaluation reports or any documentation recognizing the productivity, creativeness, innovations made and/or ethical behavior of employees or group of employees making them eligible for the PRAISE.
observations.reason: PRAISE related DVs were suspended in audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management shall strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No.2012-002 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG, JMC No. 2013-1 on the accounting and reporting guidelines for the utilization of the LDRRM Fund. Adjusting entries shall be made by the Accountant to transfer the LDRRMF to Special Trust Fund- DRRM.
observations.reason: CY 2016 unexpended LDRRMF were not transferred to STF.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant maintains a complete set of special accounts and subsidiary ledgers for the operation of public utilities and economic enterprises. Furthermore, year-end trial balances and financial statements shall be prepared for these accounts and submitted for audit as part of the schedules of the General Fund trial balance in compliance with Chapter 6 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.reason: Financial statements for CY 2016 were submitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Government of San Enrique GAD focal person prepares and submit GAD Plan and Budget as well as appropriate and utilized the minimum budgetary requirement for Gender and Development of five percent (5%) of the total budget for the year. Moreover, it is recommended that the GAD Focal Person should submit the following documents/reports relative to the CY 2014 GAD, to wit:
a. Copy of transmittal letter to the Philippine Commission on Women and reasons if the same was not transmitted to PCW.
b. The consolidated Annual GAD Accomplishment Report for CY 2014 of concerned offices prepared in accordance with what is prescribed under Joint Circular No. 2004-01 of DBM, NEDA and NCRFW.
c. Management‟s evaluation report on the existing policies of GAD.
d. Schedule of actual expenses charge to GAD CY 2014
observations.reason: GAD reports were not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Municipal Accountant immediately submit the disbursement vouchers, receipts and their supporting documents of transactions for the months of September to December 2013 to comply with COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.
observations.reason: DVs for CY 2016 were submitted upon request of the auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Drivers Trip Tickets should be completely accomplish while MROT and MRFC should be prepared and submitted to the auditor pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.
observations.reason: Monthly Report of official Travel and the Monthly Report of fuel Consumption were not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Budget Officer implement the minimum budgetary requirement for Gender and Development of five percent (5%) of the total budget for the succeeding year in compliance to Section 28 of the General Provisions of GAA FY 2013 as well as pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.reason: GAD reports were not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management submit to the COA Auditor the triplicate copy of the perfected POs within five (5) days from perfection to give them ample time to review and appropriately act. Hence, ensuring the validity of the purchases made and accuracy of the accounts affected.
observations.reason: Perfected POs were not submitted within the set period of submission.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant reviews accounting entries and analyze the loans payable Account with a negative balance of ₱131,042.61 and effect necessary adjustments to correct the account balance. Included in current year‟s audit findings.
observations.reason: The loan is still not fully paid but Loans Payable account has no balance at year end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Local Chief Executive enforce prompt settlement of audit disallowances amounting to ₱210,000.00
We also recommend adherence to prescribed accounting and auditing rules and regulations to avoid incurrence of disallowances.
observations.reason: Related documents were not turned-over by the previous auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the entries made in granting of petty cash fund by debiting Petty Cash Fund and crediting Advances to Officers and Employees. We further recommend that the required supporting documents for replenishment of petty cash fund be submitted for further evaluation. We also recommend that subsidiary ledgers be set up for each accountable officer who were granted petty cash fund.

We recommend that the management reclassify disbursement amounting to ₱113,000.00 that were initially recorded as outright expenses to other Receivables (Loans) and provide necessary subsidiary ledger per beneficiary. We also recommended that management issue demand letter to the delinquent beneficiaries for recovery of the funds invested.

We further recommend that the LGU shall implement close monitoring of these projects so as to intensify self-sustainability of this livelihood program and avoid wastage of government funds as mandated under Section 476 (4) of Republic Act No. 7160, the Local Government Code of 1991, the Planning and Development Coordinator monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development programs, projects, activities in the local government units concerned in accordance with the approved development plan.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare the reclassifying entries to take up receipts of grant from other national government agencies by debiting Government Equity (account 501) and crediting Due from other NGAs (account 416). Further, we recommend that subsidiary ledgers (SLs) be set up for each grant of loan so that these will be the basis of monitoring of repayments made by the recipients.

We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the report of Accountabilty for accountable Forms (RAAF) and consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) and submit to the Unit Auditor for verification not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month, in order to allow updated information and to easily establish the correctness of receipts and issuance of accountable forms.
observations.reason: The Municipality still did not submit RAAF during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant designate record purchase of supplies and materials first in the Inventory accounts. Deductions in the inventory accounts or expense shall be based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) prepared by the Supply officer.

We recommend that the treasurer and the municipal Accountant Designate must control the granting of cash advances to the Cash Disbursing Officer (DO) – Designate. They must see to it that only the net amount of the payrolls for a pay period should be granted to her, so as not to give chance for the accumulation of cash in the possession of the accountable officer.

We recommend that control measures in the granting of cash advances be set up and responsible municipal officials be warned that this practice is a violation of Section 89, P.D. 1445. The granting of the cash advances to an accountable officer for the disbursements of another person should be discontinued for it contravenes with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommend further that a written explanation be submitted by concerned officials who approved granting additional cash advances without liquidating the previous cash advance which defies law, rules and regulations.
